@abtink I have introduced a state machine for handling TCP connection. So now it's possible to send multiple queries without altering the buffer.
So a few words of explanation of how it works:
- `kTcpUninitialized` means that the connection is not operational
- `kTcpConnecting` means that connection establishment is in progress next query will be sent in `HandleTcpEstablishedCallback`
- `kTcpInitialized`  - means that the connection was established but is idle.
- `kTcpSendingInProgress` - means the query is in progress and we are queuing the next to the same server to be sent in `HandleTcpSendDoneCallback`

It's actually a really good question - and why this was a TODO in the first place.
It's hard to know where to pop the channel/session/link because if it's initiated by us, we don't always wait around for the service response. 
So we need to test/be able to handle:
- If we get an incoming close/end/detach, then is this in response to one we already sent? If not, we can probably just send the response frame then pop it.
- If we are sending a close/end/detach, if we pop it then we need to be able to handle additional incoming frames that are sent to it. Otherwise, do we wait until we explicitly receive the response? That can be slow and messy if there's other things going on in the connection.

I bundled it with the version and class since that's how the IPv6 implementation dealt with it. Should I just keep it to specifying ECN for now? Or should I split the DCSP, ECN, and Flow bits into separate fields?

In the update mode, the parameter value is not obtained from `mc` because we just need to use the value provided by the user to overwrite the value in `mc`.

But in the create mode, `mc` is completely blank, and we want to assemble the user's input into a complete `mc`. Considering that our parameters have a certain dependency or mutual exclusion relationship, or the parameters will be normalized before putting into `mc`, there are even some parameters that do not need user to provide explicitly, but can be dynamically completed by cli.

In order to ensure the consistency of the state and make the value of the parameter more clearly determined, we created the following principle in the create mode, that is, if a parameter has been placed in the `mc` object, then when trying to get the parameter value again, (e.g. cross-validation with other parameters) the value in `mc` shall prevail.

I do think we still need this. For the following reason:
In APPO and IMPALA, we build/preprocess the train batch along the rollout_fragment_len (or `max_seq_len` if LSTM) boundaries. This means that in case a rollout ends within an episode, this rollout's last trajectory will end up in the train batch with a zero-padded right side, thus, the bootstrapped value for this fragment is in the middle of the train batch, NOT at time-axis index -1!
So adding these two together here covers that particular case as well. It'll lead to most bootstrapped value to be at the end of the train batch rows, but some (in those cases where the rollout was done within an episode) will be located in the middle of the train batch's rows. Here: "row" means a trajectory (along the T-axis) within the (B, T, ...) train batch.

I also had this idea. What I propose is following:

Connection base class will have `codec` and `obfuscation` static attributes. Subclasses can redefine them by assigning specific `codec` and `obfuscation` classes to these attributes.
`Connection` class will just instantiate them in `__init__` and `connect` and will provide generic `_send` and `_recv` methods that will use `self._codec` and `self._obfuscation` to encode and decode packets.

```
Connection:
    codec = None
    obfuscation = NoopObfuscation
    def __init__(...):
        self._codec = self.codec()
    def connect(..):
        self._obfuscation = self.obfuscation(...)
        # somehow send "header", yet to decide how to choose if use tag from obfuscation or codec
    def _recv():
        return await self._codec.read_packet(self._obfuscation)
    def _send(self, data):
        self._obfuscation.write(self._codec.encode_packet(data))
```

Concrete classes will just redefine this 2 static attributes

```
class ConnectionTcpAbridged(Connection):
    codec = AbridgedCodec

class ConnectionTcpFull(Connection):
    codec = FullCodec

class ConnectionTcpObfuscated(Connection):
    codec = AbridgedCodec
    obfuscation = ObfuscatedIO

class ConnectionTcpMTProtoAbridged(Connection):
    codec = AbridgedCodec
    obfuscation = MTProtoIO

...
```

Here is how I usually treat the types (sort of my cheat sheet :))
- A `Neighbor` is a node we can directly talk to. `InvalidState` means it is not a neighbor (i.e. can not directly talk to it)
- `RouterTable` gives list of all routers within Thread network. Some `Router`s may be our neighbor and some may not be (e.g, two hops away).  `Router` inherits from `Neighbor` and uses `State` to indicate this. 
- A `Child` (`ChidTable`) is the list of devices own children and a `Child` is always a `Neighbor` (invalid state mean it is no longer a child).

`RouterTable` provides `GetNeighbor()` vs `GetRouter()` methods, first one returns a `Neighbor` (state is not invalid) vs second one returning a `Router` entry (may not be a neighbor).  

`ChildTable` provides `FindChild()` methods, returned entry is always a neighbor (state is not invalid). Let's caller to define a `StateFilter` to find `Child` in specific states.

`Mle` provides methods to `GetNeighbor()` (matching rloc, ext address, etc), returned value should be always a neighbor (i.e. not in invalid state). May be an entry from `ChildTable` or  `RouterTable` or one of special entries `Parent` or `ParentCandidate`.

> OK. I guess that this specifier is not compatible with setuptools as it is not listed there, is it?

I don't know the specific version but `setuptools` used in sklearn, numpy, scipy and pandas supports version specifier as least.

> Also, why do we need it? We are compatible with all (reasonable) versions except 0.22.0.

Not enough.
As you know, many python packages follow [Semantic Versioning](https://semver.org/), including scikit-learn.

If we specify `scikit-learn!=0.22.0` and v1.0.0 of scikit-learn is released, LightGBM will allow users to use v1.0.0, which can lead to break backward compatibility.
Therefore, if we follow [Semantic Versioning](https://semver.org/), we should specify libraries' version by Version specifiers.

The weird part of this is that there are two notions of Optional:

1) The target type doesn't have the Field registered to begin with.
2) The field is registered, but the user didn't explicitly define it.

This construct `required_fields`, paired with `tgt.get()`, deals with situation #1. 

--

If we go with `Optional[PexAlwaysWriteCache]`, then the call sites now must deal with both distinct notions of optionality, like this:

```python
if self.always_write_cache and self.always_write_cache.value is True:
    args.append("--always-write-cache")
if self.emit_warnings and self.emit_warnings.value is False:
    args.append("--no-emit-warnings")
```

Using `tgt.get()` is meant to avoid this clunkiness by saying "if your target type doesn't have this field in the first place, the default constructor for that `Field` will do just fine."

The core package defines ``html_static_path`` and has files in ``_static``.

Basically the previous code was just to catch the case where people were defining ``html_static_path`` and then not actually using it. I don't really think we should be working around this, since it's an easy fix (just don't define ``html_static_path``).

Also, the `test_mix_of_concrete_and_symbolic` test fails.

In this test we:
1. Create memory with 0, 2, 4, 6 offsets/indexes/memory cells as concrete values and 1, 3, 5, 7 as symbolic.
2. Assert if those concretes/symbolic addresses are really concrete/symbolic.
3. Swap indexes of concrete/symbolic memory cells and reinitialise memory bytes to concrete/symbolic values.
4. We reassert those. **Here, the test fails.**

This is because we don't delete the old symbolic mappings, so in `mem._symbols` we have old 1, 3, 5, 7 symbolic cells and 0, 2, 4, 6 new symbolic cells. **The 1, 3, 5, 7 should not be there.**

Okay, let's leave this code. I figured out how to make it recursive, but don't think rewriting it will make it much more readable, so iterative + your great comments is probably good enough.

In case you're curious, my thoughts on a recursive approach:
* The problem is that the tree of items isn't a proper datastructure, it's just a nested python dict. This means that there's no way to pop back 'up' the tree when we encounter a row of depth <= its parent.
* We could solve this by a) implementing a tree datastructure (probably overkill), or b) explicitly passing the parent links into the recursion. In pseudo-code, b roughly looks like:
   ```
   def _recursive_call(all_rows, cur_row_index, parent_stack):
       # all_rows = the csv parsed into a list of rows
       # cur_row_index = the row index we're looking at
       # parent stack = [root_item, root_child_1, root_grandchild_x, ...], all items that are parents of the current row
       cur_row = all_rows[cur_row_index]
       cur_row_depth = find_first_nonempty_row(cur_row)
       cur_item = build_item_dict(cur_row)
       if not parent_stack: # we haven't added the root yet, do so and recurse to the next row
           _recursive_call(all_rows, cur_row_index + 1, [cur_item])
       if cur_row_depth > len(parent_stack):  # we have a child of the latest parent, put it in there and recurse to the next row
           parent_stack[-1]['items'].insert(0, _recursive_call(all_rows, cur_row_index + 1, parent_stack + [cur_item]))
       else: # ignoring the error cases, we are no longer the child of the latest parent, pop up the parent stack and try again with this row
           _recursive_call(all_rows, cur_row_index, parent_stack[:-1])
   
   ```
   * Basically, recursion lets us rewrite your parent_items list to be a more intuitive object, but introduces other complexities that probably balance out the overall pain of the funcion.

For the first question: yes - nearly all of the FCompute functions that do not use random resource are compatible with graphs.
For the second question - for FCompute operators to not be compatible you need to do synchronization inside the operator - either via stream synchronize or allocation. You generally do not want to do either one of those as it really hurts performance (and the operators that in this PR I marked incompatible in this PR do just that). If you just launch a kernel (or multiple), which is the case for vast majority of the operators, then you are good and do not even need to think about graphs - it will just work.

I'm still exploring the ways of automatically testing newly added operators in order for the feature to be able to be on by default, but I do not consider this the scope of this PR, as v1.x branch is not really supposed to get many more operators (I will do that in the PR to master). Generally this would involve testing operators with `hybridize(static_alloc=True, static_shape=True)` (which generally should be tested much more as right now testing of this functionality is really limited, even though it is widely used).

<details><summary>test_merge_on_index</summary>

```python
def test_merge_on_index():
    pandas_df1 = pandas.DataFrame(
        {
            "idx_key1": [1, 2, 3, 4],
            "idx_key2": [2, 3, 4, 5],
            "idx_key3": [3, 4, 5, 6],
            "data_col1": [10, 2, 3, 4],
            "col_key1": [3, 4, 5, 6],
            "col_key2": [3, 4, 5, 6],
        }
    )

    pandas_df1 = pandas_df1.set_index(["idx_key1", "idx_key2"])
    modin_df1 = pd.DataFrame(pandas_df1)

    pandas_df2 = pandas.DataFrame(
        {
            "idx_key1": [4, 3, 2, 1],
            "idx_key2": [5, 4, 3, 2],
            "idx_key3": [6, 5, 4, 3],
            "data_col2": [10, 2, 3, 4],
            "col_key1": [6, 5, 4, 3],
            "col_key2": [6, 5, 4, 3],
        }
    )

    pandas_df2 = pandas_df2.set_index(["idx_key2", "idx_key3"])
    modin_df2 = pd.DataFrame(pandas_df2)

    for on in (
        ["col_key1", "idx_key1"],
        ["col_key1", "idx_key2"],
        ["col_key1", "idx_key3"],
        ["idx_key1"],
        ["idx_key2"],
        ["idx_key3"],
    ):
        res = modin_df1.merge(modin_df2, on=on)
        ref = pandas_df1.merge(pandas_df2, on=on)
        df_equals(res, ref)

    for left_on, right_on in (
        (["idx_key1"], ["col_key1"]),
        (["col_key1"], ["idx_key3"]),
        (["idx_key1"], ["idx_key3"]),
        (["idx_key2"], ["idx_key2"]),
        (["col_key1", "idx_key2"], ["col_key2", "idx_key2"]),
    ):
        res = modin_df1.merge(modin_df2, left_on=left_on, right_on=right_on)
        ref = pandas_df1.merge(pandas_df2, left_on=left_on, right_on=right_on)
        df_equals(res, ref)

The ``EncryptionSDKClient`` and all provided ``CryptoMaterialsManager`` are thread safe.
But instances of ``BaseKMSMasterKeyProvider`` MUST not be shared between threads,

a new  ``BaseKMSMasterKeyProvider`` per thread is sufficient.

Finally, while the ``CryptoMaterialsCache`` is thread safe, 
sharing entries in that cache across threads needs to be done carefully
(see the !Note about partition name `in the API Docs <https://aws-encryption-sdk-python.readthedocs.io/en/latest/generated/aws_encryption_sdk.materials_managers.caching.html#aws_encryption_sdk.materials_managers.caching.CachingCryptoMaterialsManager>`_).

Eg. `TdmsWriter.__init__` could look like `__init__(self, file, mode='w', version=4712, with_index_file=False, index_file=None)`, and if `with_index_file` is True and file isn't a file path, then the `index_file` parameter needs to be provided. Although that's a bit awkward that there are two parameters that are closely related and you'd need to raise an error if `index_file` was passed but `with_index_file` was False. So maybe a better API would be `__init__(self, file, mode='w', version=4712, index_file=False)`, where the `index_file` can either be a boolean, and if True then the file parameter needs to be a path and we work out what the index file path should be, or `index_file` can be an opened file.

```py

Since the ArgumentList is already single-quoted, you have to use double-quotes for the interior quotes.

`SubConverter` subclasses take care of cleaning paths in `.write()`, so I don't think we should add any more checks to subclass `launch` methods. Obviously steering this is up to you, but here's why I think these changes are safe, and get closer to deprecating `Environment.launch` altogether someday:

Four places call `Environment.launch`:
  - `graph.write`
  - `lily.showPNG`
  - `lily.showSVG`
  - `scale.show`
  - technically also `vexflow.toMusic21j.TextExternal.testCuthbertLocal`, but I've never heard of that guy

***

`graph.show` passes a hard-coded `'png'` format.
It usually receives a temp `fp` from `stream.plot()`, but a power user might pass an unexpanded path to `graph.show()`, so yes, we could add a safety check, but I would do it in `graph.write` to be analogous to `SubConverter.writeDataStream`.

***
`lily.showPNG` passes a hard-coded `'png'` format.
The `fp` passed to `launch` is the fp returned by `lily.createPNG`, which is a `pathlib.Path`, so I don't think it needs to be cleaned.

***
`lily.showSVG()`: sim.
***
`scale.show` already runs `common.findFormat()` and will only `launch` if regularized fmt == 'scala'.
The `fp` it passes to `launch` is returned from `self.write('scala')`, so since it's already been used, it's too late to clean it.

We tend to define help docs as their own target and add that as a pre-requisite to all. This is so all of the help docs in all relevant makefiles are outputted. This will work as this is the only place you're defined a concrete recipe for the target. But if it someone else decided to do the same elsewhere in future, it wouldn't work as expected. It's probably best to stick to the same pattern here for consistency and to avoid others in future copy-pasting a pattern that might not work in other contexts. One issue I have with this approach is our authentication becomes "knows the app key." The trouble is, every client application needs that same key in order to work, but we don't necessarily want every client application to be able to authenticate as any user.

In the implementation of this we have with Kolibri GNOME + Endless OS (https://github.com/endlessm/kolibri-desktop-auth-plugin/), we ended up using a separate authentication token for user logins. That's because, on Endless OS, we have a system-wide Kolibri service that multiple desktop users may talk to. So, each of those users is allowed to see the app key. Instead, we need a different key to be able to say "I am _this_ user."

Since `Predictor$predict()` accepts things like `rawscore` as keyword arguments, supporting passing something like `predict_raw_score` in `params` would require one of the following changes to `Predictor`:

* extract `rawscore`, `predleaf`, etc. in the constructor and store them as private attributes, before converting to string with `lgb.params2str()`
* accept a `params` list in `Predictor$predict()` instead of in the constructor
* accept a `params` list in `Predictor$predict()` and merge it with whatever `params` were passed into the constructor and stored at `private$params`

I think it'd be better to do this work up in `Booster` than to make any of those changes to `Predictor` to support this.

I'm just worried about there being too many options for going from text to embeddings, and that the ways they are used are mutually exclusive, so you have to change how you process your data if you want to switch between them, which breaks one of the main points of the library.

Similarly, the `CharacterEncoder` looks like a fancier version of our [`CnnEncoder`](https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/master/allennlp/modules/seq2vec_encoders/cnn_encoder.py) wrapped inside a `TokenCharactersEmbedder`.  It's great to have a better version of this, except now we have two ways of going through the code to get almost the same result.

It seems like we're getting a proliferation of abstractions.  I _think_ the goal of this one is to have something that wraps a `TextFieldEmbedder` and then a `Seq2SeqEncoder`, adding a few options along the way.  That's great, I see no issue there.  But the way it currently is, it doesn't actually use the `TextFieldEmbedder`, it just duplicates _almost_ the same functionality in an incompatible way, so we can't actually use things interchangeably anymore.

Basically we have a singleton custom resource that we have defined a `kopf.timer` for. Since this resource only exists once on the cluster we don't run into the worker problems. https://github.com/nolar/kopf/issues/642 has not been an issue for us since kopf introduced the retries. Alternatively you could just start a separate thread yourself in a `@kopf.configure` handler and do it in there.

In that `kopf.timer` handler we then iterate over all Dask resources on the cluster. We make use of [kopf indexing](https://kopf.readthedocs.io/en/stable/indexing/) here so that we don't have to query the API server in each iteration.

I double-checked the logic that generates this value. Here is the fact I collected:
1. `time` field is not filled with a real value until the bundle moves to `RUNNING` stage. This means that the `time` field will not be assigned with real value when the bundle is in `STAGED` state.
2. When frontend code tries to display the `time` field from metadata, even though `metadata.time` variable is defined, but it still hasn't been assigned with real value which leads to `typeof s === "undefined"`.
3. When frontend tries to display the `time` field using [renderDuration function](https://github.com/codalab/codalab-worksheets/blob/master/frontend/src/util/worksheet_utils.js#L6-L30), since the value of `s` is undefined, variable `y` and `d` is `NaN`. At the end the function see [Line 30](https://github.com/codalab/codalab-worksheets/blob/master/frontend/src/util/worksheet_utils.js#L30), when it concatenates `Math.round(y) + 'y' + Math.round(d) + 'd';`, since both `y` and `d` are `NaN`, the final returning result becomes `NaNyNaNd`. 

Another option to fix this could be checking `isNaN(y)` and `isNaN(d)` before the last return statement. I think checking `s` at the beginning of this function might be better as the program doesn't need to go through the rest of the logic. Let me know how you think!

The help documentation from the `auto` flag was wrong, this is not the order we always use. `auto` actually means "a sensible default for each scenario". For training it is `last -> best -> init`, but for testing, exporting, and single file inference, it's `best -> last -> fail`. So we can't have a single magical function that does the right thing. Better to either have an API like: `load_checkpoint(session, FLAGS.load, auto_order=['last', 'best', 'init'])` or keep this simple and let the callers handle the fallback logic like they used to.

V1 does not put sources in this pex, it puts sources in a sources pex, requirements in a requirements pex and the pytest pex merges both of those in:
https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/29cf9fc8b25a540f32e4a9e0aeeaaa6ea4dcc48f/src/python/pants/backend/python/tasks/python_execution_task_base.py#L163-L166
https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/29cf9fc8b25a540f32e4a9e0aeeaaa6ea4dcc48f/src/python/pants/backend/python/tasks/python_execution_task_base.py#L179-L184

In V2 we now built a pytest pex and a requirements pex and merge both of those using a PEX_PATH only entrypoint pex. I did it this way just to make breaking out the ordering of the PEX_PATH easy to see and change, ie: pytest then requirements.

We could put the sources in a Pex in v2 and there should be little perf difference. Without that, if sources change we push and pull a new source digest. With that we would create a new source pex and then pull that. I doubt the speed difference is noticeable. That said, its conceptually clear not to create a source pex since basre files are shipped with digests in V2, so that's the good reason in my mind to stick with how sources are currently handled in V2.

Instead of all this logic being in `src/c_api.cpp`, I think most of the implementation for `LGBM_BoosterGetConfig()` should live somewhere else. Similar to how `LGBM_BoosterSaveModelToString()` is defined here in `src/c_api.cpp`.

https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/cf38071b6ac7e238b1654286dbb5a9a98b2c320b/src/c_api.cpp#L2233

And then it is just a thin wrapper on `GBDT::SaveModelToString()`.

https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/a06fadfb7ac3fdb26da3d4afc061a8b976070c50/src/boosting/gbdt_model_text.cpp#L410

I also think it's desirable for any code that needs to list every parameter by name should be in the automatically-generated methods in `src/config_auto.cpp`.

So I think this should be approached as following:

1. Add a method like `GBDT::GetLoadedConfig()` which reformats `loaded_parameter_` into the appropriate format a parameters string and then initializes a `Config` object by calling `Config::GetMembersFromString()`
    - https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/cf38071b6ac7e238b1654286dbb5a9a98b2c320b/src/io/config_auto.cpp#L321
2. Add a method in `src/io/config_auto.cpp` like `Config::DumpConfigToJson()`, which returns a JSON representation of a `Config` object.

But if I was working on this, my approach would be to just write code directly in `src/io/config_auto.cpp`, get the tests into a good state, and *then* make `parameter_generator.py` write that code.
    - this will be useful if LightGBM adopts the proposal at #4887 in the future
3. Alter `LGBM_BoosterGetConfig()` to get the output of `Config::DumpConfigToJson()`.

Cython doesn't have any concept of nullptr, so you do have to pass NULL instead everywhere in Cython. The reasons to use nullptr have more to do with properly disambiguating different function signatures (and in general avoiding ambiguities in things like casting, for instance `int x = NULL` is perfectly valid but undesirable). In practice, assuming no ambiguity is present, `NULL` is either 0 or `(void *) 0` (I believe that's implementation-defined), and the result of calling a function `f(float *x)` in Cython as `f(NULL)` is equivalent to an implicit cast to a null `float *` pointer.

The upshot of all of this is that while we do have to use NULL in Cython, it's safe to use nullptr in C++ in all the corresponding places.

**DaskDF and Koalas only implements about** `55%  <https://arxiv.org/abs/2001.00888>`_ **of the pandas API**; they do not implement certain APIs that would deviate from the row-wise partitioning approach, or would be inefficient with the row-wise parallelization. For example, Dask does not implement ``iloc``, ``MultiIndex``, ``apply(axis=0)``, ``quantile`` (only approximate quantile is available), ``median``, and more. Given DaskDF's row-oriented architecture, ``iloc``, for example, can technically be implemented, but it would be inefficient, and column-wise operations such as ``apply(axis=0)`` would be impossible to implement. Similarly, Koalas does not implement ``apply(axis=0)`` (it only applies the function per row partition, giving a different result), ``quantile``, ``median`` (only approximate quantile/median is available), ``MultiIndex``, ``combine``, ``compare`` and more.

Btw, according to the [kraken v5 documentation](https://dev.twitch.tv/docs/v5/reference/streams#get-live-streams), supported values are `live`, `playlist` and `all`, but that's not true. There's also a second attribute, `broadcast_platform`, which isn't documented, but is related to reruns and IIRC, it was used by Twitch before they added the `stream_type` attribute. Both attributes have changed quite a bit over the past years after reruns were implemented by Twitch. For example, there also was the `watch_party` value which was used to indicate reruns.

Even if `stream_type` is the documented property, I am not sure if it's the right property to check. Most attribute names can be used for filtering streams via a GET parameter, like `game` for example, but `stream_type` can not be used for filtering whereas `broadcast_platform` can, which is weird. See here:
```bash
curl -s \
  -H "Accept: application/vnd.twitchtv.v5+json" \
  -H "Client-ID: phiay4sq36lfv9zu7cbqwz2ndnesfd8" \
  "https://api.twitch.tv/kraken/streams?stream_type=rerun&limit=1" | \
  jq '.streams[0] | {stream_type: .stream_type, broadcast_platform: .broadcast_platform}'
```
```json
{
  "stream_type": null,
  "broadcast_platform": null
}
```
```bash
curl -s \
  -H "Accept: application/vnd.twitchtv.v5+json" \
  -H "Client-ID: phiay4sq36lfv9zu7cbqwz2ndnesfd8" \
  "https://api.twitch.tv/kraken/streams?broadcast_platform=rerun&limit=1" | \
  jq '.streams[0] | {stream_type: .stream_type, broadcast_platform: .broadcast_platform}'
```
```json
{
  "stream_type": "rerun",
  "broadcast_platform": "rerun"
}
```

Then there's also the problem of broadcasters not setting the rerun flag while streaming a rerun. The Twitch GUI does some additional regex checks of the channel's status message (stream title) in addition to checking both `stream_type` and `broadcast_platform` attributes.

> Someone get error when trying initialize abstract class. This can break something, so should be prevented.

Initializing a base class can't break anything, the user won't even touch these classes unless he has explicitly searched for them, we don't and shouldn't be adding preventions like these because there is no reason to.

Keeping more code in the codebase, even though it can be removed is simply not a good idea, and there are a lot of people familiar with data-classes, and inheritance, and some even with abstract base classes, while not knowing about `__new__`, even some people who do have some experience with python won't know about `__new__`, because it's mostly not needed. It is quite rare that people would need to mess with it, and so we shouldn't expect most people to know about it.

> Someone want to contribute and edit this code, so firstly he need to understand it all.

If someone just wants to contribute a small fix to the codebase, they certainly shouldn't need to be familiar with every little aspect of it. While yes, if they want to understand the project and perhaps to some more work for it in the future going through the whole codebase is something that's worth doing, especially for a project that's not that big like mcstatus, but it shouldn't be a requirement.

And once again, since many python programmers don't know about `__new__`, some contributors could easily get discouraged to contribute because of the complexity of this code. Just because you know how to do something doesn't mean it should be done.

I really like the idea of the topic following the logger name, but as you said that may not be feasible, especially with how PUBHandler is built. It's designed to build up the message topic from the root topic, then the log level, then a subtopic passed in with a :: in the message. Seems tricky to modify that without changing existing behavior.

After we get past this, I was considering adding a new handler class that did use the logger name as the topic, because then the topics could follow the package hierarchy, and you could subscribe to log notifications based on where a module is in the layout of your codebase. How neat would that be!

I'm pretty sure the logger name would be available to do that - it is one of the attributes of LogRecord objects. 

But for this, as it is today, I don't care too much about what we call it, but I do think it shouldn't be blank. 

When I first started with this class, I inadvertently set the attribute `roottopic` instead of the correct `root_topic`. I got no errors (another reason I dislike direct attribute access in object APIs) but also no messages. I finally set my subscription to a blank value to start getting anything. That was confusing especially for a ZMQ newbie like myself - I didn't realize until very recently that you could have a "wildcard" subscription. 

My thinking is that if the value is populated with a default value, client code like the log watcher I built can "just work" out of the box, by subscribing to an expected value. But, we could get to the same outcome if we defaulted to a blank value, and then my log watcher could just subscribe to the empty string. I can happily make that change if you'd prefer - I just wasn't sure if "wildcard" subscriptions were to be encouraged in the ZMQ world.

Oh, and the reason I choose the string 'root' is because that is the name of the root logger in the core Python library. Could easily be something else.

Sanity check would be helpful here. This is an actual change to exception sink handling for pantsd. I don't think the original was working how we expected. 

The constructor for `Exiter` expects a function that gets set to `self._exit`. This is then used by `Exiter.exit()`.  It defaults to `sys.exit`. 

The old `daemon_pants_runner.py` code here did not account for two things:
1) `previous_exiter` may be `None`. `ExceptionSink` defaults to `None` for the class property. In practice, it's always defined because we set the value to `sys.exit` when importing `exception_sink.py`, but ideally this constructor will not rely on that assumption.
2) we were passing an `Exiter` instance to `Exiter.__init__()`, rather than a function. So, `self._exit` was being set to an `Exiter`. I think this worked because we define `Exiter.__call__()`, but does not seem intended. Instead, we should pass to the constructor the underlying `_exit` property from `previous_exiter`.

Now that each `deploy` call here is asynchronous, is there a chance that the Serve controller calls `deploy_group` and then calls `run_control_loop` before `deploy_group` finishes executing?

If so, that could be a bit problematic. The synchronous `deploy_group` is used to atomically deploy a group of deployments. This is useful in case any deployment tries to access the Serve handle of another deployment from the group during initialization.

Could we somehow keep `deploy_group` and `deploy` synchronous (or at least provide an option that makes them synchronous)?

---

For extra context, a deployment's handle is only available once the controller executes `deploy` on deployment (specifically after [this line](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/62143e8e18eadfa1894ef44f4aa97782fc223c06/python/ray/serve/controller.py#L391) finishes). However, the controller will initialize each deployment only inside `run_control_loop`. A synchronous `deploy_group` is useful since it forces the controller to execute `deploy` on a group of deployments before calling `run_control_loop` again, allowing any deployment in the group to access any other deployment's handle during initialization.

```

132130d17a (manifest-rev) manifest-rev@{0}: fetch https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr +master:refs/heads/manifest-rev: forced-update

I'm a little lukewarm about this rule, in particular when it comes to protobuf definitions that we clearly control ourselves (like the ones in tensorboard.data; and possibly in the future if we were to explicitly fork the event / summary protos and stop mirroring TF).  Had responded on the thread, ICYMI: https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/pull/4385#discussion_r530596758

I realize that it's a little hassle to have to go and update the code everywhere if we decide to change the proto, but that work isn't any different from if we'd made the proto definition a Rust struct natively and had decided to add a field, and yet we don't write all our struct literals with a trailing `..Default::default()` in case they're expanded in the future.  I think this is more or less a similar argument as described in abseil.io/tips/147 about exhaustive switch cases in C++; it seems okay to me to say that we will sign up for either 1 or 2 (3 wouldn't apply unless we were to include the mirrored TF protobufs in this as well).

I'd be more sympathetic here if we could at least make this a compile-time check or a linter check (or if Prost generated a proto-creation API that was future-proof, like a `withFoo()` builder pattern, that we could use instead), but if we can't, it seems like there's a decent chance eventually at least one case will slip through, and since we can't prevent that possibility we'll want to be aware of the possibility that a protobuf schema addition will break the Rust build even if we try to adhere to this rule.

Would be curious to hear thoughts from others and whether you think there's a particularly ironclad reason we should strive to provide protobuf changes with this kind of guarantee.  (FWIW, the only backwards-compatibility guarantee I'm aware of with protobuf is at the wire level; I am not aware of any general guarantees about backwards-compatibility for generated code when the definition changes.)

Yes, you are right! Basically there are two cases as follows:
1. bundle.request_gpus > 0
2. bundle.request_gpus == 0

In the first case, when a bundle requests for GPUs, since the logic already filters out CPU workers in [Line400-Line425](https://github.com/codalab/codalab-worksheets/blob/master/codalab/server/bundle_manager.py#L400-L425) before reaching to the sorting algorithm, adding logic to filter GPU workers out takes no effect in the final result. 

The situation that `gpu_priority` would have a real impact is in the second case when a bundle requests for CPUs only. In such a situation, the bundle can be scheduled to run on both GPU workers and CPU workers. However, since GPUs are considered as more precious computing resources than CPUs, we would want to utilize CPU workers as much as possible in the first place.

> I wonder if you could provide an example of user-defined Modin operator

@vnlitvinov 

What we usually use the user-defined operators for is to emulate lazy execution for those types of transformations that can't be written into a one or two pandas API calls (usually such transformations are performed in a for-loop).

As an example, consider that we have a dataframe with multiple string columns, and then we want to combine those columns into a single one using the specified separator. Surprisingly, but the fastest way to do so using vanilla pandas is simply writing a for-loop:

```
combining multiple string columns using different approaches in pandas:

for-loop: 17.533942513167858
df.apply(join): 21.597900850698352
df.str.cat(): 38.55254930164665
```

<details><summary>pandas code</summary>

```python
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from timeit import default_timer as timer

NCOLS = 16
NROWS = 5_000_000

df = pd.DataFrame({f"col{i}": [f"col{i}-{j}" for j in range(NROWS)] for i in range(NCOLS)})

t1 = timer()
res = df.iloc[:, 0]
for col in df.columns[1:]:
    res += "_" + df[col]
print(f"for-loop: {timer() - t1}")

t1 = timer()
res = df.apply(lambda row: "_".join(row), axis=1)
print(f"df.apply(join): {timer() - t1}")

t1 = timer()
res = df.iloc[:, 0].str.cat(df.iloc[:, 1:], sep="_")
print(f"df.str.cat(): {timer() - t1}")
```

</details>

Then when adapting this code to modin, it appears that the for-loop approach works very slow due to a lot of kernels being submitted to Ray and so causing it to overwhelm (each iteration of the for-loop will result into 3 separate kernels: 1. `df[col]`; 2. `"_" + df[col]`; 3. `res +=`). And then it appears, that the most performant approach is to submit this for-loop as a single kernel using the Reduction operator:

```
combining multiple string columns using different approaches in modin:

reduction operator: 2.6848975336179137
batch pipeline API: 2.945119895040989
for-loop: 36.92861177679151
df.apply(join): 8.54124379903078
df.str.cat(): 43.84469765238464
```

<details><summary>modin code</summary>

```python
import modin.pandas as pd
import modin.config as cfg
import numpy as np
from timeit import default_timer as timer

cfg.BenchmarkMode.put(True)
# start all the workers
pd.DataFrame([np.arange(cfg.MinPartitionSize.get()) for _ in range(cfg.NPartitions.get() ** 2)]).to_numpy()

NCOLS = 16
NROWS = 5_000_000

df = pd.DataFrame({f"col{i}": [f"col{i}-{j}" for j in range(NROWS)] for i in range(NCOLS)})

from modin.core.dataframe.algebra import Reduce

def reduction(df):
    res = df.iloc[:, 0]
    for col in df.columns[1:]:
        res += "_" + df[col]
    return res

t1 = timer()
res = Reduce.apply(df, reduction, axis=1)
print(f"reduction operator: {timer() - t1}")

from modin.experimental.batch import PandasQueryPipeline

t1 = timer()
pipeline = PandasQueryPipeline(df)
pipeline.add_query(reduction, is_output=True)
res = pipeline.compute_batch()
print(f"batch pipeline API: {timer() - t1}")

t1 = timer()
res = df.iloc[:, 0]
for col in df.columns[1:]:
    res += "_" + df[col]
print(f"for-loop: {timer() - t1}")

t1 = timer()
res = df.apply(lambda row: "_".join(row), axis=1)
print(f"df.apply(join): {timer() - t1}")

t1 = timer()
res = df.iloc[:, 0].str.cat(df.iloc[:, 1:], sep="_")
print(f"df.str.cat(): {timer() - t1}")
```

</details>

One example is a workflow that defines two unique steps, the second depending on the first. The second step expects a file from the first step as input, and will wait until everything in step one is finished before starting. Suppose that there are multiple inputs to the first step, each of which can be processed through the entire two-step workflow independently. The workflow execution could be parallelized by running both steps as multi-step stages, where a single instance of step one is scheduled to process each input, and likewise a single instance of step two processes the output from an instance of step one. With this approach, the second step instances would need to wait until all instances of step one are finished before proceeding, despite only depending on the output from one of them.

I know that if there are users that use `drf-extra-fields` and has this exact same test in their project and if they switch to `Django 4.2 + psycopg 3`, their test will fail.     def signed_session(self, session=None):
        """
        Sign requests session with the token. This method is called every time a request is going on the wire.
        The user is responsible for updating the token with the preferred tool/SDK.
        In general there are two options:
            - override this method to update the token in a preferred way and set Authorization header on session
            - not override this method, and have a timer that triggers periodically to update the token on this class
        The second option is recommended as it tends to be more performance-friendly.
        :param session: The session to configure for authentication
        :type session: requests.Session
        :rtype: requests.Session
        """
        session = session or requests.Session()
        session.headers['Authorization'] = "Bearer {}".format(self.token)

        return session

    def token(self, new_value):
        """
        :param new_value: new value to be set as the token.
        """
        self.token = new_value

The reason for the test failures is that `ConnectionManager._join_partner` relied on the contract proxy raising a `DuplicatedChannelError` to detect already existing channels. With the recent changes to the proxy this is no longer true, it may raise anything now depending on when the channel creation happened:

- a `BrokenPreconditionError` if its visible in the precondition checks already,
- a `DuplicatedChannelError` if it happens to be not yet visible in the precondition checks but already onchain
- a `RaidenRecoverableError` (gas estimation failed for unknown reason) if the channel is only visible in the pending block. 

My fix is:
- catch the relevant cases of `BrokenPreconditionError` too. That's why the subclasses are introduced.
- handle the `RaidenRecoverableError`s by checking for a new channel again. Of course, the contract proxy should handle this case itself and never raise a `RaidenRecoverableError`, but I've given up on arguing about the contract proxies at this point.

To deal with this I think we'll want to modify the StepSpec object to store a second number sequence for each step, stored as a list so that we can distinguish between levels. Then we can use this new set of numbers for display purposes.

Another thing I thought of: flow steps can have conditions attached using the "when" clause -- see the `deploy_unmanaged` flow for an example. We should probably display these to make it clear that there is conditional logic involved to determine what actually runs.

This strategy is (currently) very specific to this task and to selecting a global interpreter for a context. In particular, it doesn't fingerprint anything else about the target: only its constraints. `ResolvedJarAwareFingerprintStrategy` falls back to the target fingerprint, so is a bit more general.

_Should_ it be fingerprinting more of the target so that it could be used in more places? Yes, probably. For example, if there are linters that consume these constraints and which are fingerprinted, they should re-run when the constraints have changed... BUT, I think that those tasks are already dynamically selecting interpreters per-target, and thus aren't using this product.

All this to say: this feels like a good incremental step. Looking at the other `Task.invalidated` blocks for python tasks and seeing whether they're accounting for interpreters would be good. But I don't think it's a blocker here.

You and me both. :) However this PR seems to me a strict improvement on what came before, where the corresponding navigation was in class Entity as follows:
```
def get_variable(self, variable_name, check_existence = False):
  return self.simulation.tax_benefit_system.get_variable(variable_name, check_existence)
```

In calls like these, and whether `person` is a population or an entity:
```
person('rent', period)
```
the only way to know about the variable-entity link is to ask the TaxBenefitSystem. (A possible evolution would be to make each Entity responsible for keeping track of its own variables, in the same way that each Population is keeping track of its own values; but that's another major restructuring, and I'd argue well out of scope for this PR.)

To get a TaxBenefitSystem instance, either we explicitly hold a reference to it - which is what this PR does - or we use the one in the Simulation instance we have for almost entirely different reasons (i.e. so we can call `calculate`) - this is what was happening previously; it's a Demeter violation and has a "sneaky" feel to it.

This is related to the issue we had with the period check; passing in a Simulation to the formulas, so that they can `calculate` with it, makes sense because Simulation has a "bird's eye view" of the system; passing in an Entity (or as is now the case a Population) makes less sense, because these objects have a narrower view and are not able to do the job by themselves.

I understand that we might not want to go back to the previous formula signature where Simulation was passed in. (I'd still be curious to know more about the reasons for the change, if you remember the PRs in which it was done and where discussions might have taken place.)

And to me there is a different way forward, which would eliminate a lot of boilerplate calls from formulas, unlock a bunch of optimizations, and facilitate static dependency computation; in a nutshell, it consists of reintroducing the variable dependencies as parameters of the formulas. But that's a bigger task and needs a separate RFC, discussion and plan.

Oh, sorry, I thought you'd followed the background in #1339. This is a new feature in Windows Python 3.7. In 3.7.2, the Python executable installed in a venv-style virtual environment changed from being a full `python.exe` to being a "redirector", that relies on being run from a venv. When virtualenv copies that redirector, it breaks. In 3.7.3, in response to some bug reports (that I haven't fully reviewed) the `sys._base_executable` attribute was added to point back to the actual exe from the source environment. That gives us a pointer to the file we need to copy.

**Unfortunately**, it looks like even this isn't sufficient - because the redirector in the intermediate venv just runs the base `python.exe`, it doesn't need the Python DLLs, and so they aren't present in the venv - so we don't copy them to the virtualenv, and as a result, `python.exe` fails because a DLL it relies on isn't present. I have no idea why this error didn't show up in my manual testing - I've just started getting errors now, even though I'm doing basically the same as I was before. Maybe I had some weird `PATH` setup that obscured the problem...

Anyway, I suspect now that this approach might be dead in the water. The whole situation is an utter mess, to be honest - and it's definitely not helped by the fact that the details aren't documented and hence aren't reliable.

I don't think it's viable to simply say that we don't support running virtualenv from within a venv - tools like pipx use venv and so running something like pew or tox from pipx will hit this limitation.

One option that *might* work to handle this is to effectively do what the `--python` option does. If we find we're running from a venv, re-invoke ourselves from the underlying "base" Python. I *think* that should be reliable, but I need to do some local tests first (as CI has Python 3.7.2, which is always going to be a problem because of the lack of `sys._base_executable`). I'll test it and update this PR once I know one way or the other.

On a somewhat related note, as you mentioned `site.py`, I'd suggest that we add support for *all* of the venv "standard" attributes (`sys.base_prefix`, `sys.base_exec_prefix`, and `sys._base_executable`) in our `site.py`, so that at least end users have a standard interface they can rely on (I don't know why venv didn't use the `sys.real_prefix` attribute that was already defined by virtualenv, but it's too late to change that now). I can prepare a separate PR to do that.

This PR is currently a 1:1 translation from ADAL to MSAL. It would work no worse than before, but it also does not utilize many good features from MSAL, such as token cache.

Ideally we shall reuse MSAL's `PublicClientApplication` and `ConfidentialClientApplication` (as mentioned in the first Q&A of [this recent conversation](https://github.com/AzureAD/microsoft-authentication-library-for-python/issues/549)), but that would need a major architecture change to this repo. We discussed offline that this repo has a shelf life for less than a year, and [its replacement](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/samples/azure/azure-sdk-for-python/storage-datalake-samples/) is already available, so, we choose to use the current "1:1 translation" approach.

There is one low-hanging fruit we can grab. Define a module-wide in-memory dictionary, for MSAL to use it as an [http cache](https://msal-python.readthedocs.io/en/latest/#msal.PublicClientApplication.params.http_cache). This is required for MSAL's built-in http 429/5xx error retry to work across multiple `lib.auth(...)` runs.

```suggestion
        contextPub = msal.PublicClientApplication(client_id=client_id, authority=authority+tenant_id, http_cache=_http_cache)
```

>  If you want to use FetchContent then the directory is variable and there needs to be some mechanism for the MXNet code to find intgemm.

Essentially you need to update the intgemm cmakelists file to ensure it encapsulates the headers as part of the intgemm target. Once you do that, cmake will handle finding intgemm header files correctly. The reason that things break once you use `FetchContent` is that the `intgemm/CMakeLists.txt` is not correct.

I could think of three instances, in which the proposed splitting logic would indeed fail us.

1. Multiple options to `env` and `-S` isn't the first one:
```
#!/usr/bin/env -v -S python -u
```

2. Combined options:
```
#!/usr/bin/env -Sv python -u
```

3. Using the long option instead of the short option:
```
#!/usr/bin/env --split-string=python -u
```

If we want to correctly handle the first and second example, we, more or less, would have to expand the scope of the issue to handle all options of `env`. The third example would further add handling their respective long option forms.

```python
@dataclass
class Motd(_MotdSimplifiesMixin, _MotdTransformersMixin):
    ...  # Includes constructors

# This is NOT a dataclass    
class _MotdSimplifiesMixin:
    __slots__ = tuple()
    
     # type definition for `parsed` (as this is a mixin to Motd, which we know does contain this, so it will be available)
    parsed: list[MotdElement]
    
    ...  # This should include the `simplified` method, along with all of the individual simplifies


# Same approach for transformers class
```

I did some research on **Meta** options. Django has predefined [DEFAULT_NAMES](https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/db/models/options.py#L28) which are supposed to be the only attributes you can define within a **Meta** class.
The error if you try to define any custom attribute in Meta TypeError: 'class Meta' got invalid attribute(s): autoslugfield_slugify_function
The only way to bypass the problem is to redefine those **DEFAULT_NAMES** in a model's module where you are going to use the feature (which seems a bit cumbersome): ```py from django.db import models # ... models.options.DEFAULT_NAMES = models.options.DEFAULT_NAMES + ('autoslugfield_slugify_function',) ``` If we decided to work with Meta, the overall code would look like this: ```py """ some models.py module """ from django.db import models from django_extensions.db.models import TitleSlugDescriptionModel from pytils.translit import slugify # We have to write this line of code in each # module where we decided to use custom "slugify" feature models.options.DEFAULT_NAMES = models.options.DEFAULT_NAMES + ('autoslugfield_slugify_function',) class Author(TitleSlugDescriptionModel, models.Model): class Meta: autoslugfield_slugify_function = slugify ```
Why? This renders fine as-is.

> To allow later usage of the fetched refs space then git fetch will

and as we are already keeping remote stuff under `refs/west/*` we might as well keep whatever `west` fetches in there, because then we have it available for later use, and thus may reduce number of additional `git fetches` and hence improved performance.

Note, this is NOT about mirroring the remote refs space, simply just storing the information we anyway have fetched, so that we might avoid fetching the same again.

We are fetching the exact same amount of data with or without PR #331, the PR just have the potential to reduce later redundant fetches.

@regebro, nope. That's scaled.

You are confusing scaled with scaled relative to a location. Whereas the operation scaled is relative to the origin. To perform a scale relative to the center you would do path 
path -= 1.5 + 1.5j
path *= 2
path += 1.5 + 1.5j

That is scale and scale is by definition a *very* useful graphical operation. I think the confusion is in thinking scale cannot move elements. It totally can. I'm not sure how you would make all point relative to other points larger without moving the points around.

A rectangle at (1,1), (1,2), (2,2), (2,1)  scaled by 2 has points (2,2), (2,4), (4,4), (4,2) that's not an argument for wrongness, that's the definition of scaled in a geometric sense. All scale operations are relative to the origin. You need to do "transform(-x,-y), scale(s), transform(x,y)" to do this relative to a point but that's really just move the origin, scale, move the origin back.

Yeah I considered that possibility as well, and chose to stick with this implementation precisely because I wanted to maintain `out` as an optional parameter. However, on second though I've changed my mind and would like to require the `out` parameter in C++. Here's my reasoning: we already have different semantics in Python and C++, because in Python we create a new array for the user if one is not provided. I think it would be a less obvious, and therefore more insidious, inconsistency if the default behavior when `out` is not provided is to overwrite in place in C++ while it is to return a copy in Python. C++ users are much more likely to care about the performance impact of minimizing copies, so it's much more consistent with best practices in the language to require them to handle allocating new arrays if they need to; otherwise, they can just pass a pointer to the same array to trigger in-place overwrites.

Note that in order for this to be a good idea we'd want to make sure that all the C++ APIs for box functions behave the same way. I think that shouldn't be an issue though.

Possible implementation:

```python
base_is_an_interface = abc.is_abstract(union_base)  # I didn't check if this existed

if invalid_members:

```

True. I considered doing everything in the Fourier domain, but decided against it for three reasons. (1) this method (computing the discrete kernel) is independent of the input size so will not unduly suffer in any way for large images (although the FFT is very fast...), (2) I could closely mimic the `scipy.ndimage` implementation of the Gaussian filter using a simple kernel, (3) it allows a free choice of boundary conditions, unlike doing everything in the Fourier domain, which only allows a `wrap` type of boundary condition. Now we can in principle use `reflect` or `cval` or anything of the like too. And if you set 'truncate' to half the width/length of the image, and set `mode='wrap'` in your usage, the FIR implementation and discrete Fourier transformed version should be completely identical. ```py
@staticmethod
def _make_env_vars(function: Function, file_env_vars: Dict, inline_env_vars: Optional[Dict]) -> Dict:
    """Returns the environment variables configuration for this function
    Parameters
    ----------
    function : samcli.lib.providers.provider.Function
        Lambda function to generate the configuration for
    Returns
    -------
    dictionary
        Environment variable configuration for this function
    Raises
    ------
    samcli.commands.local.lib.exceptions.OverridesNotWellDefinedError
        If the environment dict is in the wrong format to process environment vars
    """

    name = function.name
    result = {}


    # validate and raise OverridesNotWellDefinedError
    for env_var in (file_env_vars or {}).values() + (inline_env_vars or {}).values():
        if not isinstance(env_var, dict):
            reason = """
                        Environment variables in incorrect format
                        """
            LOG.debug(reason)
            raise OverridesNotWellDefinedError(reason)

    if file_env_vars:
        parameter_result = file_env_vars.get("Parameters", {})
        result.update(parameter_result)
    
    if inline_env_vars:
        inline_parameter_result = inline_env_vars.get("Parameters", {})
        result.update(inline_parameter_result)
    
    if file_env_vars:
        specific_result = file_env_vars.get(name, {})
        result.update(specific_result)
    
    if inline_env_vars:
        inline_specific_result = inline_env_vars.get(name, {})
        result.update(inline_specific_result)

    return result
```

There was an agreement on a temporary solution (by appending aggregated metrics to each result dict first, previous comments [1](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/22099#pullrequestreview-890618542), [2](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/22099#issuecomment-1049138730).) Some considerations are
1. We don't want to directly append aggregated metrics to `results_list`. This will change `results_list` (for instance, increase its length by 1) and causes test failures.
2. (1) or (2) in the comment 1 essentially suggests appending aggregated metrics to all result dicts, since we don't know which worker will be logged. One drawback of this is duplicated aggregate metrics in `results_list`.
3. The `ResultsList` class is created for keeping the existing properties of `results_list` ad avoiding duplicate aggregated metrics.

Since you require `data` and `path` to not be `None` and since `path` only gets set in these nested if-statements, just invert the conditions and return `None`, so that all this nesting can be avoided. Also, why is the `seasons` property optional in the schema but mandatory here for returning streams (otherwise `path` is `None`)?

Example (untested):
```py
    @staticmethod
    def _get_media_data(data, key, slug):
        media = data.get(key)
        if media and media[0]["slug"] == slug:
            return media[0]

    def _get_streams(self):
        m = self.match.groupdict()
        if m["slug_live"]:
            data = self._get_api_data("channelSlug", m["slug_live"])
            media = self._get_media_data(data, "EPG", m["slug_live"])
            if not media:
                return

            self.id = media["id"]
            self.title = media["name"]
            path = media["stitched"]["path"]

        elif m["slug_series"] and m["slug_episode"]:
            data = self._get_api_data("episodeSlugs", m["slug_series"], filter=m["slug_episode"])
            media = self._get_media_data(data, "VOD", m["slug_series"])
            if not media or "seasons" not in media:
                return
            episode = media["seasons"][0]["episodes"][0]
            if episode["slug"] != m["slug_episode"]:
                return

            self.author = media["name"]
            self.category = media["genre"]
            self.id = episode["_id"]
            self.title = episode["name"]
            path = episode["stitched"]["path"]

        elif m["slug_movies"]:
            data = self._get_api_data("episodeSlugs", m["slug_movies"])
            media = self._get_media_data(data, "VOD", m["slug_movies"])
            if not media:
                return

            self.category = media["genre"]

This just adapts your implementation to downloaded images. Since there might be missing images after download, we can no longer zip and filter like you did. My idea to solve this is to an `IterKeyZipper` (conditionally zip two datapipes) with the images as reference. As zip key we can use the name of the image which is encoded in that path as well as the URL.

```suggestion
        images_dp, urls_dp, captions_dp = Demultiplexer(
            resource_dps[0], 3, self._classify_files, drop_none=True, buffer_size=INFINITE_BUFFER_SIZE
        )

        images_dp = hint_shuffling(images_dp)
        images_dp = hint_sharding(images_dp)

        urls_dp = LineReader(urls_dp, decode=True, return_path=False)
        captions_dp = LineReader(captions_dp, decode=True, return_path=False)
        anns_dp = Zipper(urls_dp, captions_dp)

        dp = IterKeyZipper(images_dp, anns_dp, path_accessor("name"), buffer_size=INFINITE_BUFFER_SIZE)
        return Mapper(dp, self._prepare_sample)
```

I haven't looked it up, but it's a silly issue. Visual C++ sees it as a `const` variable that isn't captured, so it produces an error when it's used in the lambda. And if you capture it, then you get a signed/unsigned comparison warning. Clang sees it as a compile-time constant and doesn't require capture (in fact it produces a warning which we translate to an error if we try to capture). It might have to do with the move to `constexpr` in later C++ versions, I'm not sure. But in any case it's trivial to fix by just making it a compile-time constant via `enum`.

> It's also a new concept to learn

Well, it's a new concept no matter what. It seems very likely that the default won't work for at least one 3rd party requirement, so users will need to learn how to specify the mapping.

More generally, they will need to learn about dep inference / buildgen.

> Or make it an argument to the python_requirements macro. But yea.

My concern here is how decentralized the mapping becomes. You need to scan both your `BUILD` for the `requirements.txt` and any BUILD files that might define an inlined `python_requirement_library`.

I think it's better to have it in one central, unambiguous place. 

> thinking about the documentation for this

The plan is to add a new section to https://pants.readme.io/docs/python-third-party-dependencies called "Teach Pants how to understand your import statements (recommended)", underneath the lockfile section. The JSON file would be recommended to live right next to `requirements.txt`.

I'm not very concerned about being able to teach that quickly and concisely.

Also, reminder that this will usually only need to be done once by a codebase admin. Then when new deps are added, add a mapping, which is easy to figure out based on the other entries.

This is adapted from a recipe in the[importlib_resources migration guide](https://importlib-resources.readthedocs.io/en/latest/migration.html#pkg-resources-resource-filename). The returned file name could be a temporary file (e.g. extracted from a zip). The importlib_resources-functions return a context manager to manage the lifecycle of that file. To mimic the semantics of resource_filename, I had to get rid of the context manager, so I used the ExitStack recipe to have the file available for the whole lifetime of the process and cleaned up at exit.

also, if I understand correctly both the simple and fixed charge rate LCOE models assume that the degradation rate is the same as the discount rate or the cost of capital, which is probably conservative. Deg rates are typically less than 1% while the suggested discount rate is 3%. The FCR simplification is:
```
LCOE = (FCR*CAPEX + OPEX_FIX) / YIELD + OPEX_VAR  # OPEX_VAR = zero for solar!
```
which is equivalent to: 
```
LCOE = (CAPEX + OPEX/FCR) / (YIELD/FCR)  # just use OPEX for fixed O&M
```
I believe we can do better than this by just using either the lifetime production in the denominator or using a different FCR for production:
```
LCOE = (CAPEX + OPEX/FCR) / (YIELD/FIXDEGRATE)
# where FIXDEGRATE = [1 / SUM(1 / (1+RD)**n) for n in range(N)]
# N is lifetime in years, same as used for fixed charge rate
# and RD is the degradation rate, usually 0.75%/year
```
I think this might return an LCOE with more realistic accounting for degradation?

This seems to be the only use of `getExpiredArtifacts`, so perhaps that utility could be combined here, or incorporated as a nested function?

I think this is going to miss expiring artifacts:

* call get_queue_artifacts with a page size of 1000
* call S3 for each of those 1000
* call delete_artifact for each of those 1000
* call get_queue_artifacts with a page size of 1000, offset 1000

..at this point, we've missed 1000 expired artifacts since the offset skips 1000 non-deleted artifacts.

This actually isn't the end of the world -- those artifacts would hopefully just get picked up in the next run of the expiration job -- but might be confusing when we wonder why there's a small tail of expired artifacts in the DB.

I suspect the fix would be to decrement the offset by 1 for every call to `delete_queue_artifact`.  In the success case, that will end up calling `get_queue_artifacts` with offset = 0 every time, until it returns no results, but in the failure case it will not re-try deleting S3 objects.

Separately, it seems like we should be taking a closer look at these errors instead of just ignoring them.  I'll file a followup for that.

distributed tracing has been around since the beginning.

it is supported in two ways in integrations:

1. Integrations which make calls to third party applications, these are typically "client" libraries (like `httpx`, `requests`, `grpc` client, etc).

For these integrations we want to "inject" our distributed tracing metadata into the requests being made so they are available by the downstream application to access.

2. Web server integrations (e.g. `flask`, `django`, etc) who will try to "extract" the distributed tracing metadata from the inbound requests to properly associate it's spans with the upstream services metadata.


This is how we get connected distributed traces (e.g. Service A -> Service B -> Service C all in the same trace/flamegraph).

So this is the pattern I was following, from Sven's comment earlier:
```
config:
  off_policy_estimators:
    my_is:
      type: ImportanceSampling
      ctor_kwarg1: [some value1]
    dr:
      type: DoublyRobust
      ctor_kwarg1: [some value]
      ctor_kwarg2: [some other value]
```

So the name e.g. `my_is` is the string under which the estimator's results are saved. `type` is the actual class or classpath for the estimator. For IS and WIS, this isn't really relevant, since they don't take any other arguments beyong policy and gamma.

However for e.g. DR, you can do something like this:

off_policy_estimation_methods: {

What I mean by inline requirements is the ability to have decentralized requirements via `python_requirement_library`. With this PR, a requirement must be defined in the lockfile for it to actually be used anywhere. Say you have a tiny script separate from your codebase that needs `requests`; now your whole repo depends on that.

Technically, you could still have a `python_requirement_library` defined separately in a BUILD file. But we would ignore it.

In fact, if we don't want to merge, then we should consider erroring if we detect requirements that are not covered by your requirements.txt. I could see that being really confusing to a user why after following the advice of https://www.pantsbuild.org/v2.0/docs/python-third-party-dependencies#defining-inline-requirements, the req still isn't showing up. It wouldn't be obvious that they instead must update their global `requirements.txt`.

Instead of using that big character-set, can we use \S+ (which means non-whitespace)?

```python
r"(add\s+\w+\s+\S*\s+\S*\s+(superuser|crypto|maintenance|provisioning|retrieve|test-user)|secret.*)"
```

We also should prefix both of these to make them non-capturing so:

```python
r"(?:add\s+\w+\s+\S*\s+\S*\s+(?:superuser|crypto|maintenance|provisioning|retrieve|test-user)|secret.*)"
```
Basically the `?:` means we are using the parenthesis as a logical-or and not for capturing/replaying what we match. There is a case(s) where having capturing patterns can cause breakage in Netmiko. Not sure in matters here, but for safety it is better to use non-capturing (since we don't need it).

My feeling is that if the main usecase of this class is to help users translate between dbt representations and dagster representations of an asset, we'd want to bundle it together with the asset key generating function. there are a few independent places where we'd like to be able to do this translation:

- doing the `with_attributes` call after `@dbt_assets`
- inside the compute_fn, to potentially do fancy things
- when generating AssetObservations for tests (we need to covert the upstream unique ids for the test into upstream asset keys for the observations)
- when generating an asset selection via `DbtManifestAssetSelection`

There's also a particularly rough edge when you involve key prefixes, as these can get applied post-hoc, meaning there's no automatic way of ensuring these callsites are in sync with whatever key prefix has been added. Not sure if there's any perfect solution for that bit, but it's something to be aware of

Anyway, I think it'd be an ok outcome if the user was able to create their own subclass of this object to supply their own definition for `get_asset_key_for_unqiue_id` or something, I just feel like we probably wouldn't want to call this object `DbtManifest` in that case, as it's more of a translation layer

This is a tricky scenario:

```python
from typing_extensions import Annotated
from pydantic import BaseModel, PlainSerializer

def serialize_user(user: 'User'):
    return f'User(<{id(user)}>)'

class User(BaseModel):
    friends: Annotated['User', PlainSerializer(serialize_user)]
```

The problem is that at the point in time when we want to apply `PlainSerializer` to `User` the `User` schema is a `definition-ref` schema that is not yet defined. It so happens that you can add a `serialization` onto a `defintion-ref` schema, and in fact I can't think of any constraints you can add via `Annotated` that would need to modify the model schema itself (e.g. a `AfterValidator` would wrap it, only things like `Gt` modify the actual schema).

But that doesn't work in general: if the "named schema" is a `list[int]` (via `TypeAliasType`) then it should be possible to apply constraints like `MinLen`:

```T = TypeVar('T')
MyList = TypeAliasType('MyList', Annotated['MyList[T]', MinLen(1)], type_params=(T,))```

This should produce something along the lines of  `{'type': 'list', 'items_schema': {'type': 'definition-ref', 'schema_ref': 'MyList_int'}, 'min_length': 1, 'ref': 'MyList_int'}}`.

> If this has an impact on performance, I'd suggest seek()ing the start of image data.

Yes, that would be the best place to seek to, especially if it is different from the end of the header.

The reason it will improve performance is that most OSes will opportunistically start buffering/reading a file to improve subsequential calls to read. Setting the pointer to the start of the image data before giving control back to the user allows the OS to do this while the user performs other tasks. It will likely only be a small boost, but since it is free why not :)

        return fp.read(max_bytes).decode(encoding, errors="ignore")
```

Selecting an arbitrary number of bytes could truncate content in the middle of a multi-byte character and produce errors when decoding (e.g. `b'\xe2\x99\x9c\n'` -"♜\n"- could be cut at `b'\xe2\x99` and that won't decode properly). The easiest is to ignore decoding errors so that this case doesn't raise and invalid decodings are just ignored (no "�" glyph). It may mask other decoding issues, but that just makes the previewer more resilient.

a = [ 1, #Hello

        3,

the decoder makes it as ```a = [1, 2, 3]```.

It's disputable what would be best for readability and performance here. Note that there are at least 5 alternatives that look good to me:
- `c in " \t"`
- `c == " " or c == "\t"`
- `c in [' ', '\t']`
- `c in (' ', '\t')`
- `c in {' ', '\t'}`

The first solution only works if you are sure that `c` is a single character. Note that creating a list, tuple or set has a certain cost.

Out of curiosity, I measured with `timeit` and found that in fact `set` is the fastest solution, even if you only have 2 options. I would have guessed that the effect would be only noticeable with more options. Note that you want to optimize both cases, that the condition is true and that it is false. With the set, you have always a constant time. The other solutions are slower when the condition is false, and become worse the more options you have.

**TL;DR**: Luigi isn't "managing Kubernetes" so the above tests don't make sense to test.  We're telling Kubernetes contractually what to do (eg: number of retries) and it tries to do that.  No purpose in testing what Kubernetes actually did, the output of what it actually do can be wildly different dependant on many variables and situational aspects.  Kubernetes manages the aspect of retrials, which I think is a large contrast to how Luigi works with many other providers manually re-submitting/executing/scheduling things.

Well... so @dlstadther what you're asking is for me to write tests to ensure Kubernetes is executing what you tell it to. 
 I'm trying to think of a good metaphor... but in a sense, it's like someone going to a master at their craft, lets say a baker in this example, and going into their kitchen and setting up some timers and some cameras to inspect the process at every step of the way to ensure the inputs end up with the same outputs.  I think the way to explain this as well, is Luigi isn't "managing Kubernetes" because there's no such thing really (well, sure, controllers do this, but we aren't a controller for Kubernetes).  I stripped this silly retry code that was in this contrib that attempted to fiddle with some retry logic because all of that is 100% built-into Kubernetes and makes no sense.  And thus, because we are not shepherding things in Kubernetes, we aren't "managing" kubernetes any more, we also shouldn't be testing things which are managed by Kubernetes.
 
 By defining the yaml object properly and by Kubernetes accepting that object, it has committed to _trying_ to execute that contract to the best of its ability, within the constraints you give it.  And contractually it tries to do that.  If you tell it to retry x times, it will do everything it can to ensure that happens.  However, there are various various reasons (eg: resource limits/constraints, node selectors, taints, etc) that it can't fulfill the request contractually.  Since we're defining the yaml to include a specific number of retries, kubernetes will _attempt_ to do that.  But checking if Kubernetes _did_ retry, doesn't make a lot of sense.  It either did or it didn't, that's not our responsibility to test against.  In theory, those above tests are actually unstable depending on the stability of the cluster, nodes in the cluster, resource constraints (or lack of), oomkiller happening, unable to scale up the cluster, etc.  A job could absolutely be failed, and not retry at all because it was never able to initialize because of a mismatch of nodelabels, or taints, etc.
 
 I think what might make sense here is to test that everything we tried to set, is actually set.  Eg: number of max retrials, image of the job, job definition details, etc.  That's the best thing I can think of, and I hope my explanation makes sense above.
